Questions about your data?

Email our DPO at privacy@atlas.co. We acknowledge every request and respond substantively within 30 days — sooner for time-sensitive matters.

privacy@atlas.co
/ / Cookie Policy
// Privacy & data · tracking disclosure v2.3

Cookie Policy.

EffectiveMarch 22, 2026
Last updatedMarch 22, 2026
Versionv2.3
Applies toAll Users
Read time~10 min
Status• Active
• Contents
• The short version

Plain-English summary.

We use a small number of cookies and similar technologies to run the Platform. Some are strictly necessary (you can't turn them off without breaking the Service), and some are optional (analytics, preferences). We don't use advertising cookies.

In the EU, UK, and comparable jurisdictions, we ask for your consent before setting any non-essential cookie. You can change your preferences any time from the cookie preferences center in the footer. We also respect Global Privacy Control (GPC) signals.

This summary is for convenience only. The full policy below is the legally binding version. Where the summary and the full text disagree, the full text controls.

01Introduction & Scope

This Cookie Policy explains how Atlas Talent, Inc. ("Atlas", "we") uses cookies and similar technologies on the Atlas website, web application, and related services (the "Platform"). It supplements our Privacy Policy, which describes our broader data practices.

Where a conflict exists between this Cookie Policy and the Privacy Policy regarding the collection or use of data through cookies, this Cookie Policy controls to the extent of the conflict, and the Privacy Policy otherwise governs how the resulting data is handled.

02What Are Cookies?

A "cookie" is a small text file that a website stores on your device (your browser, phone, or computer) when you visit. Cookies allow the website to recognize your device on a return visit and to store limited information about your preferences or activity. Cookies can be:

  • First-party — set by the website you are visiting (in our case, atlas.co).
  • Third-party — set by another domain that our site loads, such as an analytics or identity-verification provider.
  • Session — deleted when you close your browser.
  • Persistent — remain on your device until they expire or until you delete them.

Cookies are not the only technology used for these purposes. See § 5 (Similar Technologies) for other tracking mechanisms that are subject to the same consent and preferences rules.

03Cookie Categories

We organize the cookies we set into four categories. The category determines whether we need your consent before setting the cookie in jurisdictions that require opt-in for non-essential cookies.

3.1Strictly necessary.

Essential for the Platform to function — authentication, session management, CSRF protection, load balancing, security, and consent storage. These cookies cannot be turned off in our systems; they are exempt from consent under the EU ePrivacy Directive (Art. 5(3) exception).

3.2Functional.

Remember your preferences to personalize the Service — your preferred language, timezone, currency, UI theme, and recently viewed profiles. Optional; require consent in the EU/UK.

3.3Analytics.

Help us understand how the Platform is used in aggregate — which features work, where users get stuck, what performance looks like. We use PostHog (self-hosted in the EU) as our only analytics provider. Optional; require consent in the EU/UK.

3.4Marketing.

We do not use advertising or retargeting cookies. We do not participate in cross-context behavioral advertising. The only "marketing"-adjacent cookies we use are opt-out/opt-in state cookies for our own newsletter and product-update communications.

04Cookies We Use

The table below lists every cookie we set, what it does, its category, who sets it, and how long it lasts. Third-party cookies link to the provider's own policy where applicable.

Name Purpose Category Party Duration
atlas_session Maintains your signed-in session. Stores a signed session identifier; no personal data. Strictly necessary First-party Session
atlas_auth Long-lived authentication token for "stay signed in" functionality. Rotated on login. Strictly necessary First-party 30 days
atlas_csrf Cross-site request forgery protection. Prevents malicious sites from performing actions on your behalf. Strictly necessary First-party Session
atlas_consent Stores your cookie consent choices so we remember them on return visits. Strictly necessary First-party 12 months
cf_bm Cloudflare bot management. Detects and mitigates automated attacks. Strictly necessary Third-party (Cloudflare) 30 minutes
__cf_bm_atlas Cloudflare rate limiting and request fingerprinting. Strictly necessary Third-party (Cloudflare) 30 minutes
atlas_locale Remembers your preferred interface language. Functional First-party 12 months
atlas_tz Remembers your timezone for correct time display in messages and logs. Functional First-party 12 months
atlas_theme Remembers your UI theme preference (light/dark). Functional First-party 12 months
atlas_currency Remembers your preferred display currency. Functional First-party 12 months
atlas_recent Remembers recently viewed Candidate profiles to populate your "Recent" panel. Functional First-party 30 days
ph_* PostHog product analytics. Aggregate usage metrics, feature adoption, performance. No cross-site tracking. Analytics Third-party (PostHog, self-hosted EU) 13 months
atlas_session_id Anonymous session identifier for performance monitoring (RUM). Analytics First-party Session
atlas_newsletter Remembers whether you have opted in or out of our product newsletter. Marketing (own-product only) First-party 24 months

05Similar Technologies

We use a small number of technologies that are not cookies but serve similar purposes and are subject to the same consent rules:

  • Local storage and session storage. Browser-native storage used to cache the UI and improve load performance. Treated as strictly necessary for the Service to function.
  • Tracking pixels. We do not use third-party advertising pixels. We use a small first-party performance beacon to measure page-load times in aggregate.
  • Device fingerprinting. Our fraud-prevention systems (via our identity providers, Persona and Onfido) use limited device-characteristic fingerprinting during verification only. This is disclosed in the Privacy Policy and is used strictly for anti-fraud purposes. We do not use fingerprinting for analytics or advertising.
  • WebSockets & server-sent events. Used for real-time features (messaging, notifications). These are not tracking technologies but are mentioned for completeness.

06Preferences Center

You can manage your preferences at any time from the cookie preferences center, accessible from the footer of every page. The panel below is a representative example; on the live Platform it reflects your current choices.

Cookie preferences.
Strictly necessary cookies.Always on
Authentication, security, session management, and consent storage. These cannot be disabled — the Platform will not function without them.
Functional cookies.
Remember your preferences (language, timezone, theme, currency, recent profiles). Optional.
Analytics cookies.
Help us understand product usage in aggregate. PostHog, self-hosted in the EU. No cross-site tracking.
Marketing cookies.
Only used to remember your newsletter and product-update preferences. We do not run advertising or retargeting cookies.

07Browser Controls

Most browsers let you review and delete cookies, block cookies from specific sites, or block third-party cookies entirely. Blocking cookies may break parts of the Platform that rely on cookies (particularly authentication). Browser help pages for the most-used browsers:

  • Chrome: Settings → Privacy and Security → Third-party cookies
  • Safari: Preferences → Privacy → Manage Website Data
  • Firefox: Settings → Privacy & Security → Cookies and Site Data
  • Edge: Settings → Cookies and site permissions → Manage and delete cookies

08Do Not Track & Global Privacy Control

Do Not Track (DNT). DNT is a deprecated browser header that we do not rely on because the signal is ambiguous and has no agreed meaning across browsers. We do not treat DNT as a binding consent signal.

Global Privacy Control (GPC). We respect the Global Privacy Control signal as specified at globalprivacycontrol.org. When we detect a GPC signal from your browser, we treat it as a valid opt-out of sale/sharing of personal information under the CCPA/CPRA and as a consent withdrawal for analytics and functional cookies under GDPR. This applies on a per-device basis; to ensure the signal persists across devices, set GPC in each browser you use.

09Third-Party Cookies

Where we allow third-party cookies, the third party is bound by a data-processing agreement with Atlas that restricts the cookie's use to the purposes disclosed in this Policy. Currently, the third parties that may set cookies on our domain are:

  • Cloudflare — security, bot management, rate limiting (strictly necessary). See Cloudflare's cookie policy.
  • PostHog (self-hosted in the EU) — aggregate product analytics (analytics, requires consent). See PostHog's privacy policy.

We do not embed third-party cookies for advertising, social sharing, or tracking purposes.

10Regional Variations

10.1EU / EEA & United Kingdom.

Under the EU ePrivacy Directive (Directive 2002/58/EC, as amended) and the UK PECR (Privacy and Electronic Communications Regulations 2003, as amended), we obtain opt-in consent before setting any non-essential cookie. Strictly necessary cookies are exempt from this requirement. Consent is granular by category; we keep a record of your choices and re-request consent when the purposes or providers materially change.

10.2California (CCPA / CPRA).

California residents have the right to opt out of the "sale" or "sharing" of personal information. We do not sell or share for cross-context behavioral advertising, so no opt-out is needed for that purpose. The cookie preferences center remains available to limit non-essential cookies.

10.3Other jurisdictions.

For Users in jurisdictions without specific cookie-consent rules, we apply an opt-out model by default: non-essential cookies are set, and you can disable them at any time from the preferences center. You can always choose to apply EU-style opt-in by using the "Reject all" option on first visit.

11Updates to This Policy

We may update this Cookie Policy to reflect changes in the cookies we use or in applicable law. For material changes to the cookies we set (for example, a new vendor category or a material change in purpose), we will request fresh consent before applying the change to existing Users. The current version is identified at the top of this page; past versions are archived indefinitely.

12Contact

Questions about this Cookie Policy should be directed to privacy@atlas.co. For information about our broader data practices, see the Privacy Policy. To submit a data-rights request, see § 11 of the Privacy Policy.

• Related policies
See also.
Privacy
Privacy Policy.

Our broader data practices, including legal bases, retention, your rights, and regional addenda.

Read →
Vendors
Subprocessors List.

Every third-party vendor that processes user data, including cookie providers named above.

Read →
Governing document
Terms of Service.

The foundational agreement governing Platform use. Section 13 incorporates this Cookie Policy.

Read →
v2.3 · current
Mar 22, 2026
Minor. Decommissioned Mixpanel analytics cookies (mp_*). Added GPC respect language in § 8. Updated § 9 third-party list.
v2.2
Jan 15, 2026
Added fingerprinting disclosure (§ 5). Added local/session storage treatment.
v2.1
Oct 03, 2025
Migrated analytics provider to PostHog (self-hosted EU). Removed all Google Analytics cookies.
v1.0 – v2.0
Q2 – Q3 2025
Archived. Email privacy@atlas.co for past versions.

Questions about cookies?

Email privacy@atlas.co. For general data questions or to exercise any of your rights, the Privacy Policy is the canonical source.

privacy@atlas.co
/ / Acceptable Use Policy
// Governing document · conduct policy v2.1

Acceptable Use Policy.

EffectiveMarch 02, 2026
Last updatedMarch 02, 2026
Versionv2.1
Applies toAll Users
Read time~14 min
Status• Active
• Contents
• The short version

Plain-English summary.

Don't lie on Atlas — about who you are, what you've done, or who did your work. Don't cheat on assessments or help anyone else cheat. Don't harass, threaten, or exploit other Users. Don't scrape, reverse-engineer, or circumvent the Platform. Don't use Atlas to commit fraud or route payments around our fee.

Some violations result in an immediate permanent ban with no warning — we call these zero-tolerance. Others start with a warning. Everyone has the right to appeal.

This summary is for convenience only. The full policy below is the legally binding version. Where the summary and the full text disagree, the full text controls.

01Introduction & Application

This Acceptable Use Policy (the "AUP") sets out the conduct and content rules that apply to all Users of the Atlas Platform. The AUP is incorporated by reference into the Terms of Service (see § 14) and is binding on every User regardless of role (Client, Candidate, or prospective User), geography, or account status.

We apply this AUP consistently and in proportion to the violation. We also apply it to our own employees and contractors in their use of the Platform. No one is above the rules.

02Severity Framework

Violations are categorized into three severity tiers. The tier determines the baseline enforcement response, which may be adjusted upward or downward based on the factors at § 11.2.

Zero tolerance
Immediate permanent ban. No warning. Reported to law enforcement where applicable. Examples: CSAM, violent threats, fraud schemes.
Severe
Typically a permanent ban; appeal available. Examples: sustained harassment, major fraud, mass scraping, anti-cheat circumvention.
Standard
Progressive discipline: warning → suspension → ban. Examples: spam, minor profile inaccuracies, isolated AUP breaches.

03Prohibited Content

You may not upload, post, transmit, or otherwise make available on the Platform any Content that:

3.1Violates law.Zero tolerance

Is unlawful in any material jurisdiction in which you, the recipient, or Atlas is located — including content that violates securities, tax, export, or sanctions laws, or facilitates the violation of such laws by others.

3.2Sexualizes minors.Zero tolerance

Child Sexual Abuse Material (CSAM), content that sexualizes minors, or any content that grooms, exploits, or endangers a minor. We report all such content to the National Center for Missing & Exploited Children (NCMEC) as required by 18 U.S.C. § 2258A and cooperate fully with law enforcement. Accounts are permanently banned on first detection.

3.3Promotes violence or self-harm.Zero tolerance

Content that incites, glorifies, or provides instructions for real-world violence, terrorism, mass-casualty events, or self-harm. Threats of violence against identifiable individuals, including Atlas personnel, are also zero-tolerance.

3.4Is hateful or discriminatory.Severe

Content that attacks a person or group on the basis of race, ethnicity, national origin, caste, religion, gender, gender identity, sexual orientation, disability, age, or any other legally protected characteristic.

3.5Doxxes or invades privacy.Severe

Content that publishes or threatens to publish another person's private information — home address, unlisted phone number, government ID, financial account details, or similar — without lawful justification and without consent. Doxxing the references, family members, or associates of another User is treated identically.

3.6Is deceptive.Severe

Content designed to deceive other Users, including deepfake representations of any person, fabricated work portfolios, fraudulent credentials, impersonation of real companies or brands, or manipulated screenshots presented as genuine.

3.7Is spam or low-quality at scale.Standard

Content posted in bulk for promotional or SEO purposes rather than substantive Platform use — duplicative proposals sent to unrelated jobs, mass-copied profiles, or automated content submissions.

04Prohibited Conduct

4.1Harassment and threats.Severe

You may not harass, threaten, intimidate, or bully any person through the Platform. This includes unwanted contact after a clear request to stop, targeted hostile messages, stalking behavior, and coordinated harassment. Clients and Candidates alike are protected regardless of the commercial context.

4.2Impersonation.Severe

You may not misrepresent your identity or affiliation. Do not claim to be another person; do not claim to work for or represent a company you do not; do not clone another User's profile. Using a pseudonym is permitted for Candidates in jurisdictions where that is customary, but your verified legal identity must be on file with Atlas.

4.3Fraud and financial abuse.Zero tolerance

You may not use the Platform to: commit payment fraud; knowingly accept stolen payment credentials; engage in money laundering or structured transactions; operate a pyramid or MLM scheme through the Platform; or use Atlas to collect funds intended for charitable or political causes without authorization. Fraud schemes are reported to Stripe, Wise, and applicable law enforcement.

4.4Unauthorized AI assistance in assessments.Zero tolerance

Candidates may not use AI assistants, answer keys, offscreen collaborators, pre-recorded clips, virtual cameras, voice clones, or any similar method to complete or influence any portion of the Atlas assessment stack (English test, Interview 1, Interview 2, or any future assessment). Attempting to do so results in a permanent ban of all associated accounts, forfeiture of pending payouts where applicable, and disclosure to Clients with whom you are actively engaged.

4.5Misleading representations to other Users.Severe

You may not make material misrepresentations that would cause another User to enter into or continue a Contract they would not otherwise enter. Examples: falsifying hours worked, concealing subcontractors, claiming delivered work that was not delivered, or misrepresenting that you are a company when you are not.

4.6Spam and unwanted messaging.Standard

You may not send unsolicited bulk messages, repeat-send identical messages after being blocked, or use the Platform to distribute third-party commercial messages. Platform-generated Contract-related messaging is permitted.

05Platform Integrity & Security

⚠ Platform integrity · conspicuous clause
Anti-cheat circumvention is severe.

The Atlas assessment stack depends on the integrity of the signals we collect — face-match, gaze tracking, audio analysis, and network telemetry. Any attempt to defeat, evade, spoof, or degrade these signals is a severe AUP violation, whether for yourself or on behalf of another. This includes the use of virtual cameras, voice clones, screen-sharing assistants, injected browser extensions, or any other technique that produces signals not representative of your genuine real-time performance. See also Interview Recording & Biometric Consent Policy.

5.1Unauthorized access.Severe

You may not access, attempt to access, or assist another person in accessing any part of the Platform that is not intended for your use. This includes accessing the accounts of other Users, administrative interfaces, backend systems, or data feeds.

5.2Scraping and automation.Severe

You may not crawl, scrape, or systematically extract content or data from the Platform, whether by automated tool, undocumented API, or human processing at scale. Bulk extraction of Candidate profiles, Client lists, or transaction history is a severe violation. Narrow, good-faith academic or security research may be eligible for a license under the Vulnerability Disclosure Policy.

5.3Reverse engineering.Severe

You may not reverse-engineer, decompile, disassemble, or otherwise attempt to derive the source code of the Atlas software, except to the extent such activity is expressly permitted by applicable law notwithstanding this prohibition.

5.4Infrastructure abuse.Severe

You may not launch denial-of-service attacks, consume disproportionate resources, introduce malware, exploit vulnerabilities (outside the Vulnerability Disclosure Policy safe harbor), or interfere with other Users' use of the Platform.

5.5Security testing.Standard

Security research is welcomed within the scope and safe harbor of the Vulnerability Disclosure Policy. Testing outside that scope — including on Production user data — is a violation regardless of intent.

06Commercial Conduct

6.1Non-circumvention.Severe

The substantive rule is set out at Terms of Service § 12. This AUP reinforces it: attempts to move an Atlas-originated relationship off-platform for the purpose of evading our fee are a severe AUP violation in addition to a material breach of the Terms.

6.2Off-platform payments.Severe

Candidates may not solicit payment for Platform-originated work through channels outside Atlas. Clients may not route payment for Platform-originated Contracts through channels outside Atlas. This is a severe violation because it both breaches the Terms and undermines the buyer and seller protections that Atlas provides.

6.3Rate cartels and collusion.Severe

Candidates may not coordinate with other Candidates to fix rates, allocate clients, or refuse to deal with particular Clients. Clients may not coordinate with other Clients to fix rates, blacklist Candidates, or engage in other conduct that may violate antitrust law.

6.4Manipulation of ratings and reviews.Severe

You may not buy, sell, exchange, or coerce reviews or ratings; create fake accounts to review yourself or others; or post ratings that do not reflect a genuine engagement. Incentivizing positive reviews in exchange for anything of value is prohibited.

07Intellectual Property

7.1Copyright and trademarks.Severe

You may not upload, display, or distribute Content that infringes any copyright, trademark, trade secret, right of publicity, or other intellectual-property right. Repeat infringers will have their accounts terminated.

7.2DMCA takedowns.Standard

If you believe your copyrighted work appears on the Platform without authorization, follow the notice procedure in our DMCA & Copyright Policy. False or bad-faith takedown notices are subject to 17 U.S.C. § 512(f) liability and may result in account action against the sender.

7.3Use of Atlas marks.Standard

You may reference Atlas by name and use our name in truthful statements ("I work through Atlas," "I hire through Atlas"). You may not use our logo, wordmark, or other trademarks in a manner that implies endorsement or partnership beyond the ordinary User relationship.

08AI & Automated Assistance

The use of AI on the Platform requires transparency. Different uses carry different rules:

8.1AI in assessments.Zero tolerance

As stated at § 4.4, the use of any AI to answer assessment questions, simulate speech, or otherwise produce signals during an Atlas assessment is zero-tolerance.

8.2AI in Contract work.Severe

Candidates may use AI tools to assist work performed under a Contract only with the express knowledge and consent of the Client. Material undisclosed use of AI in deliverables — for example, submitting AI-generated code, writing, or research as your own craftsmanship without disclosure — is a severe violation and may constitute fraud under § 4.3.

8.3AI in platform content.Standard

You may use AI to help draft messages, proposals, and profile content, provided the substance reflects your genuine abilities, qualifications, and intent. Profiles generated wholesale by AI with little or no human oversight are a standard violation.

09Export Controls & Sanctions

You may not use the Platform in violation of applicable export-control or sanctions laws, including the U.S. Export Administration Regulations (EAR), the International Traffic in Arms Regulations (ITAR), and the comprehensive sanctions administered by OFAC. Atlas does not permit engagements with comprehensively-sanctioned jurisdictions (currently, Cuba, Iran, North Korea, Syria, and the Crimea, Donetsk, and Luhansk regions of Ukraine) or with persons or entities on the SDN List.

Clients are responsible for ensuring that the work they engage a Candidate to perform does not require an export license that has not been obtained, and for any industry-specific controls (for example, ITAR work requires US-person status and controlled facilities that Atlas does not provide).

10Reporting Violations

If you believe another User has violated this AUP:

  • In-product: use the "Report" control available on profiles, messages, and Contracts. This is the fastest route for routine violations.
  • Email: send details to trust@atlas.co. Please include the User identifier, the nature of the violation, and any supporting evidence.
  • Urgent safety issues: for credible threats of violence or self-harm, and for CSAM, email trust-urgent@atlas.co. This inbox is monitored 24/7.

We treat reports confidentially as between the reporter and the subject where reasonably possible. We do not disclose the identity of a reporter to the subject unless required by law or by the reporter's own consent. Retaliation against a good-faith reporter is itself a severe AUP violation.

11Enforcement

11.1Actions we may take.

Depending on the severity and circumstances of a violation, we may take any of the following actions, singly or in combination:

  • Remove specific Content
  • Issue a written warning
  • Temporarily restrict specific features
  • Suspend the account for a defined period
  • Permanently ban the account and all associated accounts
  • Withhold pending payouts pending investigation (subject to applicable law and a good-faith basis)
  • Refer the matter to law enforcement
  • Pursue civil remedies including injunctive relief and damages

11.2Factors we consider.

In determining the appropriate response, we consider: the severity tier of the violation; the User's history on the Platform; whether the violation was willful or inadvertent; whether harm has occurred and its magnitude; whether the User has cooperated with our investigation; whether remediation is possible; and any applicable legal obligations.

11.3Payout holds.

We will hold pending payouts in connection with an active investigation only where we have a reasonable, good-faith basis to believe the funds relate to the violation under review. Holds are not a punitive measure; they are limited to the amount reasonably related to the issue, and they are released with prejudgment interest where a hold is ultimately determined to have been unwarranted.

12Appeals

Every enforcement action against your account can be appealed. To appeal:

  1. Within 30 days of receiving the enforcement notice, email appeals@atlas.co, reference the incident identifier, and provide any context or evidence you believe we did not consider.
  2. Appeals are reviewed by a person different from the person who made the original decision.
  3. We acknowledge appeals within 2 business days and provide a substantive decision within 14 business days.
  4. If you remain dissatisfied, the appeal can be escalated under the Dispute Resolution Policy, and ultimately under § 19 of the Terms of Service.

Zero-tolerance decisions can be appealed on the basis that the zero-tolerance categorization itself was incorrect (for example, misidentification), but not on the basis that the conduct — if it occurred as we concluded — should have been treated under a lower tier.

13Contact

Questions about this AUP, or requests for clarification in advance of particular conduct, can be directed to trust@atlas.co. Reports of suspected violations should follow the procedure at § 10.

• Related policies
See also.
Governing document
Terms of Service.

Section 14 incorporates this AUP. Section 12 is the non-circumvention clause reinforced at § 6.1.

Read →
Process
Dispute Resolution Policy.

Our five-stage dispute process, including the appeals escalation path referenced at § 12 of this policy.

Read →
Safe harbor
Vulnerability Disclosure.

Safe harbor for security researchers acting in good faith. Covers the carve-outs from §§ 5.2 and 5.5.

Read →
v2.1 · current
Mar 02, 2026
Substantive. Added Section 8 (AI & Automated Assistance) with explicit zero-tolerance rule for AI in assessments. Refined severity framework at § 2.
v2.0
Nov 10, 2025
Substantive. Major restructure. Introduced severity tiers and appeals mechanism. Added Section 9 (Export Controls & Sanctions).
v1.3
Aug 04, 2025
Clarified scraping carve-out for Vulnerability Disclosure (§ 5.2). Added rate-cartel and review-manipulation rules.
v1.0 – v1.2
Q2 2025
Archived. Email legal@atlas.co for past versions.

Questions about this policy?

Email trust@atlas.co for policy questions or to report a violation. For appeals against an enforcement decision, see Section 12.

trust@atlas.co
/ / Interview Recording & Biometric Consent
// Privacy & data · biometric disclosure and consent v2.0

Interview Recording
& Biometric Consent.

EffectiveApril 10, 2026
Last updatedApril 10, 2026
Versionv2.0
Applies toCandidates
Read time~16 min
Status• Active
• Contents
• The short version

Plain-English summary.

To verify you're really you, and to keep our assessment process honest, Atlas records your face, voice, and gaze patterns during ID verification and interview rounds. We convert those recordings into mathematical vectors (a face-geometry template and a voice print) that we use only to match you against your own verified identity and to detect cheating.

Before any of this happens, we ask you to separately and affirmatively consent. You can withdraw consent at any time. We delete your biometric templates 90 days after your last assessment — more protective than what any law requires. We never sell, lease, license, or share biometrics with third parties for any commercial purpose.

This summary is for convenience only. The full policy below is the legally binding version. Where the summary and the full text disagree, the full text controls.

01Introduction & Scope

This Interview Recording & Biometric Consent Policy (the "Policy") describes how Atlas Talent, Inc. ("Atlas", "we") records audio, video, and related data during ID verification and assessment interviews, and how we collect, use, store, and destroy the biometric identifiers and biometric information derived from those recordings.

This Policy applies only to Candidates. Clients do not undergo biometric verification. This Policy supplements, and does not supersede, the Privacy Policy — where a conflict exists between the two regarding biometric data, this Policy controls.

This Policy functions as the written notice and release required by the Illinois Biometric Information Privacy Act (BIPA, 740 ILCS 14 § 15(b)) and analogous state laws. Your affirmative consent to this Policy — captured separately before the first biometric collection — constitutes the written release those laws require.

02What We Record

Recording occurs in four specific contexts during the Candidate verification and assessment flow. We do not record outside these contexts.

2.1Identity verification.

We capture a still photo of your government-issued identity document and a short liveness sequence (a brief video of you following on-screen instructions) to confirm the document photo matches the live human in front of the camera. This prevents a static photo from being used to spoof verification.

2.2English test.

During the English assessment we record video, audio, screen activity, gaze direction, and head-pose data. The purpose is to score your spoken and comprehension abilities and to detect anomalies inconsistent with you answering the test independently (for example, reading from an offscreen source).

2.3Interview 1 (general) and Interview 2 (role-specific).

During each AI-conducted interview we record the same categories as above, plus network signals used to detect virtual-camera injection, voice-substitution software, and other assessment-integrity threats. The recording is later reviewed by your assigned Atlas recruiter.

2.4Voice intro.

When you record your on-platform voice and video introduction, we capture the audio and video you choose to submit. You can re-record as many times as you wish before submitting; only the submitted version is retained.

03Biometric Identifiers We Collect

A "biometric identifier" is a measurement of a unique biological characteristic that can identify a specific individual. We collect the following biometric identifiers from the recordings described in § 2:

3.1Face-geometry vector.

A mathematical representation of the geometry of your face — the relative positions and distances between facial features such as eyes, nose, mouth, and jaw — encoded as a numerical vector. This vector is used to determine whether the person on camera during a later interaction is the same person whose ID was verified.

3.2Voice print.

A mathematical representation of vocal characteristics — fundamental frequency, formant patterns, and spectral features — derived from audio recordings. Used to detect voice substitution (for example, an offscreen collaborator speaking while a different Candidate appears on camera).

3.3Gaze direction and head-pose vectors.

Time-series data representing where you are looking and how your head is positioned during an assessment. Used to detect reading from offscreen notes or a second monitor. These vectors do not uniquely identify you on their own; they are used only in combination with your face-geometry vector to attribute the signal to you.

3.4Keystroke and tab-switching cadence.

Patterns in how you type and navigate during an assessment. Used only for anti-cheat anomaly detection and not as a standalone identifier.

04Purposes (Exclusive List)

We collect the biometric identifiers listed at § 3 only for the purposes set out in this Section. This is an exclusive list. If we ever wish to use biometric data for any purpose not on this list, we will first obtain fresh, affirmative consent for the new purpose.

  • Identity verification. Confirming that the person completing an assessment, recording an intro, or entering a live meeting with a Client is the same person who completed the government-ID verification step.
  • Anti-cheat integrity. Detecting substitution, collusion, and other behaviors that would undermine the integrity of an assessment. This is how we keep your test score meaningful.
  • Fraud prevention. Detecting accounts created with stolen or fabricated identities, and identifying patterns of multi-account abuse.
  • Investigation of specific incidents. Where we have a reasonable basis to investigate a specific suspected violation of the Terms of Service or Acceptable Use Policy, we may review recordings and biometric data relevant to that investigation.

05What We Do Not Do

• Conspicuous clause · what we never do

We do not, and commit not to, do any of the following with your biometric data:

  • We do not sell, lease, trade, or license biometric identifiers or biometric information. BIPA § 15(c) prohibits this; we honor that prohibition globally.
  • We do not use biometrics for advertising — ever, in any form.
  • We do not use biometrics for "emotion analysis", mood inference, personality profiling, or similar pseudo-scientific applications.
  • We do not disclose biometrics to Clients. Your biometric templates never leave Atlas's systems. A Client who hires you never has access to your face-geometry vector or voice print.
  • We do not use biometrics as a training dataset for any foundation or general-purpose AI model.
  • We do not use biometrics to surveil you after an assessment ends. The anti-cheat monitoring window closes when the session closes.

06Retention & Destruction

Biometric identifiers are retained under the following schedule, which is more protective than any law currently requires of us:

  • Face-geometry vectors: retained for ninety (90) days from the date of your most recent assessment or Platform interaction, then permanently destroyed.
  • Voice prints: retained for ninety (90) days from the date of your most recent assessment, then permanently destroyed.
  • Gaze and head-pose vectors: retained for thirty (30) days from the date of the assessment, unless flagged as part of a specific investigation under § 4, in which case the retention extends to the duration of the investigation plus 30 days.
  • Keystroke cadence: retained for thirty (30) days from the date of the assessment.
  • Raw video and audio recordings (from which biometric vectors are derived): retained for one hundred eighty (180) days from the date of the recording, then permanently deleted.

"Permanently destroyed" means the data is deleted from all production systems and is removed from backups within the standard backup-rotation cycle (currently, 35 days). After the cycle completes, no copy remains. We do not retain biometric identifiers after account closure beyond these periods, even where other categories of account data may be retained for longer under tax or accounting law.

07Storage & Security

Biometric data is stored with controls at or above the standards we apply to the most sensitive categories of data we handle:

  • Encryption at rest using AES-256 with keys managed in a dedicated Key Management Service.
  • Encryption in transit using TLS 1.3 with certificate pinning for service-to-service communication.
  • Access restricted to a named list of authorized personnel performing specifically-scoped functions. Every access is logged, reviewed weekly, and audited quarterly.
  • Logical isolation: biometric templates are stored in a separate data store from profile data, with separate credentials and network access-control.
  • No local-device storage: biometric templates are never cached on Candidate or Client devices.
  • Breach notification: in the event of a personal-data breach involving biometric data, we notify affected Candidates and relevant supervisory authorities within 72 hours of discovery, consistent with GDPR Art. 33.

08Third-Party Access

We rely on a small number of specialized subprocessors for aspects of identity verification and assessment scoring. Each is bound by a data-processing agreement that restricts biometric-data use to specifically-authorized purposes and prohibits any secondary use.

  • Persona (identity verification) — processes your ID photo and liveness sequence; retains only as long as needed to return a verification result.
  • Onfido (document verification backup) — processes ID-document images; retains only as long as needed to return a verification result.
  • LiveKit (video infrastructure for interviews) — transmits audio/video streams during interviews; does not store recordings after the session.
  • AssemblyAI / Deepgram (speech transcription) — transcribes audio for scoring; does not retain audio after transcription.

The full and current list of subprocessors is maintained at the Subprocessors List. We do not disclose biometric data to any other third party except: (a) where you have provided specific written consent for that disclosure; (b) where we are legally compelled by a valid subpoena, court order, or equivalent; or (c) in connection with a business transfer subject to the restrictions at § 7.4 of the Privacy Policy.

09Consent & Release

⚠ Biometric consent · conspicuous clause
Your separate and affirmative consent.

Before any biometric data is collected from you, we present this Policy in full and ask you to:

  1. Confirm that you have read this Policy;
  2. Confirm that you understand what is being collected, for what purposes, for how long, and who has access;
  3. Provide your affirmative written consent to collection, storage, and use consistent with this Policy; and
  4. Execute a written release authorizing Atlas and its subprocessors named at § 8 to process your biometric identifiers and biometric information for the exclusive purposes listed at § 4.

Your consent is separate from your acceptance of the Terms of Service and the Privacy Policy; it cannot be bundled with other consents. Your consent is affirmative: it requires an express action (such as ticking a checkbox) and cannot be inferred from inaction, scrolling, or continued use.

If you decline to provide biometric consent, you will not be able to complete the verification and assessment process, and therefore cannot list a profile on Atlas. This is a consequence of our anti-cheat design; it is not a penalty.

10Withdrawal of Consent

You may withdraw your biometric consent at any time. To withdraw:

  1. Visit Account Settings → Privacy → Biometric Consent on the Platform, or email privacy@atlas.co with your Candidate ID.
  2. Atlas acknowledges withdrawal within two (2) business days and completes deletion of all biometric identifiers within seven (7) business days.
  3. A confirmation notice is sent when deletion is complete, with a timestamp and reference identifier for your records.

Withdrawal of consent does not affect the lawfulness of processing that occurred before withdrawal. Withdrawal will typically result in the inability to complete further assessments or continue an active Platform engagement that depends on assessment re-verification; Atlas will explain the consequences in the withdrawal confirmation flow.

11Two-Party Recording Notice

Several U.S. states (including California, Connecticut, Delaware, Florida, Illinois, Maryland, Massachusetts, Michigan, Montana, Nevada, New Hampshire, Oregon, Pennsylvania, and Washington), and the laws of many other jurisdictions, require that all parties to a recorded conversation consent to the recording. Atlas interview recording is fully disclosed and consented to before any recording begins. By proceeding with an assessment, you consent to the recording as described in this Policy.

Atlas personnel, AI systems, and our subprocessors named at § 8 may be "parties" to the recording for the purposes of applicable wiretapping statutes. No undisclosed third party listens to or observes your assessments.

12Jurisdictional Addenda

Specific U.S. states and other jurisdictions have laws that address biometric data or facial recognition with particular specificity. We comply with each of them; the cards below summarize the key provisions.

• Illinois · critical
BIPA (Biometric Information Privacy Act).
740 ILCS 14

Requires written informed consent before collection (§ 15(b)), destruction on the earlier of purpose-fulfillment or 3 years (§ 15(a)), prohibits sale and profit (§ 15(c)), and imposes a reasonable security standard (§ 15(e)). Private right of action with $1,000/$5,000 per-incident statutory damages.

• Texas · critical
CUBI (Capture or Use of Biometric Identifier).
Tex. Bus. & Com. Code § 503.001

Requires prior notice and consent, prohibits sale without consent, mandates destruction within a reasonable time after the purpose for collection expires (and no later than 1 year after the purpose is fulfilled). Enforcement by the Texas Attorney General only (no private right of action).

• Washington
Biometric Privacy Act.
RCW 19.375

Prohibits "enrolling" a biometric identifier in a database for a commercial purpose without notice, consent, or a purpose-limited need. Our enrollment is notice-and-consent-based. Enforcement by the Washington AG.

• Portland, OR
Facial Recognition Ordinance.
Portland City Code 34.10

Prohibits the use of facial-recognition technology by private entities in "places of public accommodation" in Portland. Atlas assessments are conducted from the Candidate's home or chosen private location and are not "places of public accommodation"; however, Portland-resident Candidates may opt out of facial-recognition matching and complete identity verification through an alternative process.

• New York City
AI Bias Audit Law.
NYC Local Law 144 of 2021

Where an Automated Employment Decision Tool is used for a hiring decision that materially impacts an NYC-based candidate, an annual independent bias audit is required, and candidates must receive 10 business days' notice of AI use. Atlas does not make automated hiring decisions; Clients do, with their own AEDTs. To the extent our scoring outputs feed a Client's AEDT, the Client is responsible for Local Law 144 compliance for its own hiring process.

• California
CCPA / CPRA Sensitive PI.
Cal. Civ. Code § 1798.121

Biometric information is "sensitive personal information." We collect only for the purposes listed at § 4 and do not use for secondary purposes, which qualifies for the § 7027(m) exception from the right to limit use. California residents retain all other CCPA rights.

• Colorado
Colorado Privacy Act.
C.R.S. 6-1-1301 et seq.

Biometric data is sensitive data requiring opt-in consent for processing. Our consent flow satisfies this.

• EU / EEA
GDPR Article 9.
Regulation (EU) 2016/679

Biometric data used to uniquely identify a natural person is a special category of personal data. Processing is permitted under Art. 9(2)(a) (explicit consent); that is the basis we rely on. Lead supervisory authority: Autoriteit Persoonsgegevens (Netherlands).

• United Kingdom
UK-GDPR.
Data Protection Act 2018

Parallel to EU-GDPR Art. 9. Explicit consent is our processing basis. Complaints may be filed with the UK Information Commissioner's Office (ICO).

• Brazil
LGPD.
Lei n° 13.709/2018

Biometric data is sensitive personal data under Art. 5(II) requiring specific, highlighted consent (Art. 11). Our consent flow satisfies these requirements.

13Access to Your Recordings

You have the right to access copies of recordings we have made of you. To request access:

  1. Email privacy@atlas.co with your Candidate ID and the specific recording(s) you wish to access.
  2. We verify your identity using at least two factors (the email on file plus a recent account action).
  3. We deliver the requested recordings via an authenticated download link within fourteen (14) calendar days. Delivered files are password-protected and expire after 30 days.

You may also request correction, deletion, or restriction of your recordings subject to the rights at § 11 of the Privacy Policy.

14Changes to This Policy

We may update this Policy from time to time. Because this Policy embodies your biometric-consent release, we treat any substantive change as requiring a fresh, affirmative consent. You will be asked to re-consent before any further biometric processing under the new version. Non-substantive changes (formatting, typo corrections) may be made without re-consent. The current version is identified at the top of this page; prior versions are archived indefinitely.

15Contact

Questions about this Policy, or to exercise any right described herein, should be directed to our Data Protection Officer:

Johan Vermeulen · Data Protection Officer
Atlas Talent, Inc.
privacy@atlas.co

EU Representative (Art. 27 GDPR):
Atlas Talent EU B.V.
Herengracht 123, 1015 BG Amsterdam, Netherlands

• Related policies
See also.
Privacy
Privacy Policy.

Our broader data-handling framework. Section 9 cross-references this Biometric Consent Policy.

Read →
Governing document
Terms of Service.

Section 6.2 (Candidate vetting) references the consent flow described here.

Read →
Conduct policy
Acceptable Use Policy.

Attempts to defeat the biometric anti-cheat stack are governed by AUP Sections 4.4 and 5.

Read →
v2.0 · current
Apr 10, 2026
Substantive. Major restructure to comply with latest BIPA class-action guidance (7th Circuit). Added NYC Local Law 144 addendum. Tightened retention to 90 days for face/voice vectors.
v1.2
Jan 22, 2026
Added Colorado Privacy Act addendum. Clarified subprocessor list.
v1.0 – v1.1
Q3 2025
Archived. Email privacy@atlas.co for past versions.

Questions about biometrics?

Email our DPO at privacy@atlas.co. We treat biometric questions as highest priority and aim to respond within two business days.

privacy@atlas.co
/ / Dispute Resolution
// Audience-specific · process document v2.0

Dispute Resolution Policy.

EffectiveMarch 02, 2026
Last updatedMarch 02, 2026
Versionv2.0
Applies toClients + Candidates
Read time~13 min
Status• Active
• Contents
• The short version

Plain-English summary.

Disputes go through a five-stage ladder. Stage 1 is direct: you and the other party talk it out. If that fails, Platform Mediation, then Recruiter Review, then the Atlas Dispute Panel. Only if all four internal stages are exhausted does the matter escalate to binding arbitration under Terms of Service § 19.

Each stage has a named decider, published timelines, and a written decision. Funds in escrow are held neutrally until the dispute resolves. Neither party is disadvantaged for raising a dispute in good faith.

This summary is for convenience only. The full policy below is the legally binding version. Where the summary and the full text disagree, the full text controls.

01Introduction & Scope

This Dispute Resolution Policy (the "Policy") describes how Atlas Talent, Inc. ("Atlas") facilitates the resolution of disputes between Clients and Candidates arising from or relating to engagements initiated through the Platform. The Policy is the process specification referenced at § 19 of the Terms of Service — the required steps before any arbitration may proceed.

This Policy applies to all Users who are party to a Contract on the Platform. It does not create contractual obligations that override the Contract between the Client and Candidate; where the Contract is clear and does not conflict with Atlas Terms, the Contract controls the substantive outcome.

02What's Covered

This Policy covers disputes arising from:

  • Disagreements about hours logged or work delivered under a Contract;
  • Quality of deliverables, including whether they meet the agreed specification;
  • Payment, including rate calculations, bonuses, and milestone releases;
  • Refund requests and escrow disputes;
  • Intellectual property ownership of deliverables produced under a Contract;
  • Professional conduct during an engagement, including communication breakdowns, harassment complaints, and safety concerns;
  • Alleged breaches of the Contract by either party.

This Policy does not cover: (a) disputes unrelated to Platform engagements; (b) claims that can only be adjudicated by a court or regulator (for example, criminal complaints); (c) claims for injunctive relief in IP or trade-secret matters (see ToS § 12 and § 19); or (d) claims that fall within a regulatory appeal process (for example, tax disputes).

03Five-Stage Framework

The dispute process is a ladder. A dispute escalates one stage at a time; a party cannot skip stages. If the dispute is resolved at any stage, no further stages are needed. If the deadline at any stage passes without resolution, the dispute automatically escalates.

1
Direct resolution.
48 hours

Either party raises the issue directly in the Contract thread and proposes a resolution. Atlas provides a structured template that frames the issue neutrally: what happened, what the proposing party wants, why.

This stage exists because most disagreements are misunderstandings, and most misunderstandings resolve within a business day once articulated clearly.

Decider: Client & Candidate · Output: Written resolution in-thread, or automatic escalation at 48h
2
Platform mediation.
5 business days

An Atlas mediator (a Trust & Safety team member not otherwise involved with either account) reviews the Contract, reads both positions, and facilitates a structured conversation toward a voluntary resolution. The mediator does not impose an outcome at this stage.

If both parties agree to a resolution, it is documented and closed. If no agreement is reached, the mediator prepares a factual record for Stage 3.

Decider: Neutral Atlas mediator (non-decisional) · Output: Voluntary resolution, or factual record escalated to Stage 3
3
Recruiter review.
10 business days

The Candidate's assigned Recruiter — who has the deepest context on the Candidate's skills and the original match — reviews the dispute and issues a written recommendation. The Recruiter has authority to release funds, reverse milestones, adjust hours in good faith, and recommend account-level actions under the AUP where warranted.

Either party may accept the recommendation (which closes the dispute) or reject it and escalate.

Decider: Assigned Recruiter · Output: Written recommendation with reasoning; binding if accepted by both parties
4
Dispute Panel.
20 business days

A three-person panel composed of: (i) an Atlas Trust & Safety Lead; (ii) a senior Atlas Operations team member; (iii) a senior Atlas member from outside Trust & Safety and Operations. No panel member may have had prior involvement in the matter.

The panel reviews the full record, any additional submissions from the parties (limited to 2,000 words per side plus exhibits), and issues a written decision by majority. The decision includes findings of fact, reasoning, and the operational outcome (release of funds, refund, close of Contract, account action, etc.).

Panel decisions are final within the Atlas process but do not foreclose external remedies under Stage 5.

Decider: Three-person Atlas Dispute Panel (majority rules) · Output: Written decision; operationally binding
5
Binding arbitration.
Per JAMS rules

Only after exhausting Stages 1 – 4 may a party initiate binding arbitration under Terms of Service § 19. Arbitration is administered by JAMS under its Streamlined Arbitration Rules, before a single arbitrator, in San Francisco, California.

The outcome at Stage 4 is a factor the arbitrator may consider but is not binding. The arbitrator's decision is binding on all parties, subject only to the narrow grounds for vacatur under 9 U.S.C. § 10.

Consumer carve-out. EU and UK consumers retain the right to local-court resolution (see ToS § 19).

Decider: JAMS arbitrator · Output: Binding arbitration award

04Dispute Types

While the five-stage framework applies to all disputes, specific dispute types have additional procedural details. The cards below summarize the most common categories.

Hours tracking.
Most common

Client challenges hours logged by a Candidate under an hourly Contract, or Candidate challenges a Client's reduction of logged hours.

Atlas time-tracking data (activity level, screenshots if enabled, keystroke cadence during logged hours) is the primary evidence. The 7-day lock window applies: challenges must be raised within 7 days of the hours being logged, or they are deemed accepted.

Evidence: Time-tracker logs, activity signals, in-thread communications
Work quality.
Common

Client claims deliverables do not meet the agreed specification. Candidate claims the specification was met and Client is requesting out-of-scope revisions.

The written Contract (including statement of work, acceptance criteria, and milestones) is the primary evidence. Industry-standard quality is considered only where the Contract is silent.

Evidence: Contract terms, deliverables, in-thread approvals, industry standards (secondary)
Refund requests.
Common

Client requests a refund of fees paid. Full or partial refund is decided with reference to the Refund Policy, the stage of the engagement, and the nature of the underlying grievance.

Evidence: Payment records, milestone records, work-delivered evidence, Refund Policy eligibility criteria
IP ownership.
Rare, high-stakes

Dispute over ownership of work product. The default under the Atlas standard Contract is that IP vests in the Client upon full payment, with Candidate background-IP carve-outs. Modifications to that default must be in writing.

IP disputes may be escalated to arbitration faster (skipping to Stage 4 from Stage 1) by mutual consent, given their complexity.

Evidence: Written Contract, executed addenda, prior art, Candidate portfolio, commit history where applicable
Conduct & safety.
Urgent path

Harassment, discrimination, threats, or other AUP violations within a Contract. These are moved to a fast-track path: Stage 1 is typically skipped, and safety-related claims go directly to Stage 2 or Stage 3 at Atlas's discretion.

See the Acceptable Use Policy for the severity framework applied in conduct disputes.

Evidence: Messages, recordings (if any), witness statements, pattern evidence from other users
Off-platform poaching.
Escalated

Claim that a party circumvented the Platform in violation of ToS § 12. Because these claims involve Atlas directly (as the party entitled to the conversion fee), they follow a separate enforcement path documented at conversions@atlas.co.

Evidence: Platform interaction records, correspondence, timing analysis, direct evidence of off-platform payment where available

05Evidence Standards

Atlas applies the following evidence standards across all stages and dispute types:

  • Contemporaneous records control. Messages, deliverables, milestones, and time-tracker logs created during the engagement carry more weight than accounts provided after a dispute arises.
  • Written terms control. Where the Contract addresses an issue clearly, the Contract is followed. Implied terms and industry standards are considered only where the Contract is silent.
  • Burden of persuasion. The party asserting a change to the status quo (for example, seeking a refund or seeking to reduce logged hours) bears the burden of persuading the decider that the change is warranted. The standard is preponderance of the evidence — more likely than not.
  • Spoliation presumption. Where a party destroys, alters, or withholds evidence within their control (for example, deleting messages relevant to the dispute), the decider may draw an adverse inference about what that evidence would have shown.
  • Confidentiality is not waived. Submission of evidence to Atlas's internal dispute process does not waive confidentiality, privilege, or the attorney-client relationship for purposes of subsequent arbitration or litigation.

06Escrow During Disputes

When a dispute is raised, funds held in escrow for the affected Contract are:

  • Held neutrally — not released to either party — until the dispute is resolved, unless both parties provide written release instructions.
  • Segregated from Atlas's operating accounts, consistent with the Escrow Terms.
  • Interest-accumulating where the holding period exceeds 30 days and the applicable escrow account accrues interest. Accumulated interest is distributed consistent with the final decision.
  • Released automatically consistent with the Stage 3 or Stage 4 decision, unless an arbitration demand has been filed within 30 days of the Stage 4 decision, in which case funds are held pending the arbitration outcome.

For hourly Contracts, the 7-day hours-lock mechanism operates independently of dispute resolution. A dispute raised after the lock period runs under this Policy but does not retroactively unlock the hours; a correction, if due, is effected as a reversal entry in the following payout cycle.

07Confidentiality

Dispute process records are confidential as between Atlas and the parties. Atlas will not disclose: (a) the existence of a dispute to anyone not party to it; (b) evidence submitted during the dispute; or (c) Stage 2 – 4 decisions — except (i) where disclosure is necessary to implement the decision (e.g., informing a third-party escrow agent), (ii) where disclosure is legally compelled, or (iii) with the consent of the party whose information is to be disclosed.

Neither party may publicly share confidential details of a dispute (including by posting a public review during its pendency). Public reviews are moderated during dispute pendency. Statements permitted under whistleblower or similar laws are preserved.

08Decision Records

Stage 3 and Stage 4 decisions are recorded in writing and delivered to both parties. Each decision includes:

  1. A statement of the issue in dispute;
  2. A summary of each party's position;
  3. The findings of fact and the evidence on which they rest;
  4. The reasoning for the outcome;
  5. The operational result (release/withhold funds, account action, etc.); and
  6. Information about the next stage, if applicable.

Atlas retains decision records for seven (7) years after closure for audit, regulatory, and quality-assurance purposes, consistent with the Retention Schedule.

09Cross-Border Issues

Atlas engagements routinely involve Clients and Candidates in different jurisdictions. Three cross-border considerations apply:

  • Governing law of the underlying Contract. Unless the Client and Candidate specify otherwise in writing, Contracts generated through the Platform are interpreted under the laws of the Client's jurisdiction, applied neutrally by the Atlas process. Mandatory consumer-protection and worker-protection laws of the Candidate's jurisdiction are preserved.
  • Language of proceedings. Stages 1 – 4 are conducted in English. Where a party is not comfortable in English, Atlas arranges written translation of decisions at our own cost; oral translation at mediations is arranged on reasonable advance request.
  • Witnesses and documents abroad. Atlas has no subpoena power; cooperation with evidence requests is voluntary. Where a party refuses to produce evidence within their control, the spoliation presumption at § 5 applies.

10Finality & Appeals Within the Process

Within the Atlas process, the Stage 4 Dispute Panel decision is final. It is not subject to appeal within Atlas. A party dissatisfied with a Stage 4 decision has the following options:

  1. Accept the decision as the final outcome of the Atlas process;
  2. Initiate arbitration under Stage 5, within ninety (90) days of the Stage 4 decision; or
  3. Pursue a remedy available outside the arbitration clause, including small-claims court actions, regulatory complaints, or the consumer-protection carve-outs at ToS § 19.

11Relationship to Terms of Service § 19

This Policy operates as the procedural specification for the informal-resolution requirement at ToS § 19. Completion of Stages 1 – 4, or the elapse of the applicable time limits without resolution, satisfies the informal-resolution prerequisite and permits a party to proceed to Stage 5 arbitration.

Where a party initiates arbitration without first attempting the Atlas process, the arbitrator may — at Atlas's request or sua sponte — stay the arbitration pending completion of the Atlas process, or may decline to proceed until the condition precedent is satisfied.

Nothing in this Policy limits either party's right to seek injunctive relief in court under ToS § 19 for IP infringement, misappropriation of trade secrets, or breach of ToS § 12 (Non-Circumvention).

12Contact

To raise a dispute, use the in-product "Dispute" control in the Contract thread. To appeal a closed dispute, email disputes@atlas.co with the dispute identifier. For questions about this Policy, email legal@atlas.co.

• Related policies
See also.
Governing document
Terms of Service.

Section 19 contains the arbitration clause and class-action waiver that this Policy operationalizes.

Read →
Payment terms
Escrow Terms.

How funds held in escrow are managed, including during an active dispute (see Section 6 here).

Read →
Refunds
Refund & Chargeback Policy.

Eligibility rules for refunds, which feed into refund disputes under Section 4 here.

Read →

Questions about this policy?

To raise a dispute, use the in-product control. For questions about the process, or appeals of a closed dispute, email disputes@atlas.co.

/ / Refund & Chargeback
// Audience-specific · payment terms v1.8

Refund & Chargeback Policy.

EffectiveFebruary 14, 2026
Last updatedFebruary 14, 2026
Versionv1.8
Applies toClients
Read time~12 min
Status• Active
• Contents
• The short version

Plain-English summary.

A refund is a voluntary return of funds through Atlas. A chargeback is an involuntary reversal through your credit-card network — they are different things with different rules.

We refund Platform Fees when an engagement is cancelled before work has begun, and Candidate payments consistent with the outcome of the Dispute Resolution Policy. We do not refund for services already rendered.

Filing a chargeback for services the Candidate actually performed — "friendly fraud" — is a breach of these Terms. We contest illegitimate chargebacks with full representment evidence, and repeat offenders have their accounts closed.

This summary is for convenience only. The full policy below is the legally binding version. Where the summary and the full text disagree, the full text controls.

01Introduction & Scope

This Refund & Chargeback Policy (the "Policy") sets out the rules governing refunds of Platform payments and Atlas's handling of credit-card chargebacks. It applies to Clients paying through the Platform and supplements the Terms of Service, the Escrow Terms, and the Dispute Resolution Policy.

Candidate payouts (funds flowing from Atlas to Candidates) are not "refunds" within the meaning of this Policy. Candidate-side issues with payouts are handled under the Terms of Service and the escrow mechanics.

02Refund vs. Chargeback

Two different mechanisms produce a reversal of funds. They have different rules and different timelines:

  • Refund — a voluntary return of funds initiated through Atlas's internal process. Atlas is the decision-maker. Decisions reference this Policy and, where relevant, the Dispute Resolution Policy.
  • Chargeback — an involuntary reversal initiated by you with your card issuer, processed through the Visa, Mastercard, Amex, or Discover network. Your card issuer is the decision-maker, following network rules. Atlas participates as the merchant by accepting or contesting the claim.

Refund is the preferred mechanism for any dispute within Atlas's process. A chargeback filed without first raising a refund request is likely to be contested, because Atlas will have had no opportunity to resolve the matter internally.

03When Refunds Are Available

Refund eligibility depends on the Contract type and the stage of the engagement. The matrix below summarizes the defaults; specific situations may vary.

Scenario Hourly Contract Fixed-Price Contract Platform Fee
Cancelled before Candidate accepts Full refund Full refund (escrow released) Full refund
Cancelled before Candidate starts work Full refund (any funded escrow released) Full refund (escrow released) Full refund
Cancelled mid-engagement, no deliverable yet Pro-rata: pay for hours logged + accepted Negotiated partial release via Dispute Pro-rata on Candidate pay released
Cancelled after milestone delivered Pay for logged hours; no refund of paid hours Milestone funds released to Candidate No refund of fees earned
Quality dispute (bona fide) Dispute Resolution outcome Dispute Resolution outcome Refunded if Client-side remedy granted
Atlas-caused cancellation (e.g., Candidate fraud) Full refund of unearned hours Full refund of unearned milestones Full refund
Duplicate/erroneous charge Full refund within 3 business days Full refund within 3 business days Full refund

3.1Hourly Contracts.

Candidates log hours through Atlas's time tracker. You have a seven (7) day window after hours are logged to dispute them before they automatically lock. Disputed hours within the window are resolved under the Dispute Resolution Policy.

Once hours lock and the corresponding payout is released to the Candidate, those hours are not refundable by Atlas. A Client claim after the lock window is a dispute under the Dispute Resolution Policy, not a refund request — the outcome may result in a credit for a future engagement, but Atlas will not claw funds back from a Candidate's account absent a Stage 3 or Stage 4 decision under that Policy.

3.2Fixed-Price Contracts.

Fixed-price Contracts are funded into escrow. Funds are released to the Candidate only on milestone acceptance or on expiry of the acceptance window following milestone submission. Refunds are available:

  • Before Candidate begins work — full refund of escrow and Platform Fee on Client request.
  • After Candidate begins but before submission — escrow held pending resolution under the Dispute Resolution Policy; partial refund common.
  • After submission, before acceptance — if you reject the submission, the matter enters the Dispute Resolution Policy; escrow held pending.
  • After acceptance — funds released; refunds treated as disputes, not refunds, and resolved under the Dispute Resolution Policy.

3.3Platform Fee refunds.

Where the underlying Contract is fully refunded before any work is performed, the Platform Fee is refunded in full. Where a partial refund is issued, the Platform Fee is refunded on a pro-rata basis. Where a Contract concludes with work actually performed, the Platform Fee is earned and is not refundable.

04When Refunds Are Not Available

Refunds are not available in the following circumstances:

  • The Candidate has already delivered and you have accepted the deliverable;
  • The deliverable meets the Contract specification but you have changed your mind;
  • You have used the delivered work in your own business, publication, or product;
  • The Contract was completed and the hours-lock or acceptance window has closed;
  • The refund request is filed more than one hundred twenty (120) days after the payment date, except where the Dispute Resolution Policy provides a longer window;
  • Tax-related refunds where the tax has already been remitted to the applicable authority (in which case you must seek recovery from the authority directly);
  • Fees paid to third-party providers (Stripe, Wise, escrow agents) that have been earned by those providers.

05How to Request a Refund

  1. Open the affected Contract in your Atlas dashboard and click "Request refund". Provide the reason, the scope of the refund requested (full or partial, in specific line-items), and any supporting evidence.
  2. Atlas acknowledges the request within two (2) business days. Your assigned Recruiter and the Candidate are notified simultaneously, consistent with the Dispute Resolution Policy.
  3. Where the request is uncontested — the Candidate agrees to the refund in whole or part — the refund is processed within five (5) business days of agreement.
  4. Where the request is contested, it enters the five-stage Dispute Resolution process. Atlas's final refund decision, if any, is issued as part of the Stage 3 or Stage 4 outcome.

06Refund Timing & Methods

Approved refunds are returned to the original payment method used for the transaction:

  • Credit/debit card (Stripe-processed) — refunded within 5–10 business days of approval; actual receipt depends on your card issuer.
  • Bank transfer (ACH, SEPA, wire) — refunded within 7–15 business days.
  • Atlas Credit — in certain circumstances, refunds may be issued as Atlas Credit applicable to future Platform engagements, at your election. Credit does not expire.

Refunds are issued in the original currency of payment. Currency conversion losses arising from exchange-rate movements between payment and refund are not recoverable from Atlas; we are not a currency broker.

07Partial Refunds

Partial refunds reflect the portion of the engagement where work was not delivered or where delivered work did not meet the specification. Determination of the partial refund amount is by:

  1. Mutual agreement between you and the Candidate, in any amount;
  2. Stage 3 Recruiter recommendation, which the parties may accept;
  3. Stage 4 Dispute Panel decision, which is operationally binding; or
  4. Stage 5 arbitration award, which is legally binding.

08Platform Fee Treatment

The Platform Fee is earned in proportion to the Candidate-pay released through the Contract. If $1,000 of Candidate-pay is released through a $1,100 invoice ($100 Fee), and then $400 is refunded to you, the Fee refunded is $40, preserving the 10% ratio on the net $600 retained by the Candidate.

In cases of Candidate fraud or serious AUP violation, Atlas may refund the Platform Fee in full at its discretion, regardless of the proportional formula above.

09Chargebacks

A chargeback is initiated by you with your card issuer. Your issuer transmits the dispute to the card network (Visa, Mastercard, Amex, Discover), which transmits it to Atlas through our acquiring processor (Stripe). Atlas then has a defined window — typically 7–14 calendar days — to respond with representment evidence.

Chargebacks are categorized by reason code. The most common codes that apply to marketplace services are:

  • Visa 13.1 · Merchandise/Services Not Received. Used where the cardholder claims the paid-for services were not provided.
  • Visa 13.3 · Not as Described / Defective. Used where the cardholder claims the services were materially inconsistent with the Contract.
  • Visa 10.4 / Mastercard 4837 · Fraud — Card-Not-Present. Used where the cardholder claims the charge was unauthorized.
  • Mastercard 4853 · Cardholder Dispute. Broad category including quality, cancellation, and not-received claims.
  • Mastercard 4855 · Goods or Services Not Provided. Analogous to Visa 13.1.

10Our Chargeback Response

When we receive a chargeback, we:

  1. Notify you and the Candidate within one business day of receipt, and ask both parties for any information that would support or rebut the claim.
  2. Assemble representment evidence: the Contract, messages, time-tracker logs with activity signals, deliverable receipts, acceptance records, and where relevant, portions of assessment recordings evidencing Candidate identity.
  3. Submit a defended response to the card network through Stripe within the applicable network deadline.
  4. Continue the underlying Dispute Resolution process where the factual disagreement is legitimate (for example, a genuine quality dispute). Winning the chargeback does not resolve the underlying dispute on the merits.

A successful representment reverses the chargeback; an unsuccessful representment results in final debiting of the disputed amount from Atlas's account, which Atlas may seek to recover from the Candidate in accordance with the Terms of Service.

11Illegitimate Chargebacks ("Friendly Fraud")

⚠ Chargebacks for services received · conspicuous clause
Filing a chargeback for work actually performed is a breach.

A chargeback where the Client actually received the services in question, and the underlying dispute has not first been raised through the Dispute Resolution Policy, is "friendly fraud" — a breach of these Terms. Consequences include:

  • Full defense of the chargeback with representment evidence;
  • Account suspension pending investigation;
  • Recovery of chargeback-related costs (card network fees currently $15–$25 per chargeback, plus administrative costs) from the filing Client under the Terms of Service;
  • Permanent ban for repeat or egregious patterns of illegitimate chargebacks;
  • Reporting to the relevant card network's merchant-protection programs (Visa VDMP / Mastercard ECM).

Nothing in this Policy limits your legal right to raise a legitimate chargeback for genuine fraud, non-delivery, or failure-to-conform. This clause applies to the distinct category of chargebacks filed in bad faith after services were received in conformity with the Contract.

12Regional Addenda

12.1EU / EEA — Consumer Rights Directive.

EU consumers are entitled to a 14-day cooling-off period for distance-concluded service contracts under Directive 2011/83/EU, Articles 9–16, subject to the exception at Article 16(a): the right of withdrawal does not apply where the service has been fully performed and performance began with the consumer's prior express consent and with the consumer's acknowledgement that the right of withdrawal would be lost once the service was fully performed.

Atlas engagements typically fall within this exception because the Client's initiation of a Contract constitutes prior express consent to performance commencement. Atlas presents an explicit acknowledgment checkbox before Contract initiation for EU consumers, and retains the acknowledgment as evidence.

12.2United Kingdom — Consumer Rights Act 2015.

UK consumers are entitled to services performed with reasonable care and skill, for a reasonable price (where no price is agreed), and within a reasonable time (where no time is agreed), under sections 49–52 of the Consumer Rights Act 2015. Where these statutory rights are not met, a consumer is entitled to a repeat performance, a price reduction, or — in specified circumstances — a refund.

12.3California — Credit Card Reversals.

California residents have rights under the California Civil Code regarding credit-card disputes. Nothing in this Policy limits those rights. Refund requests from California residents are handled under the same framework as other US requests; chargebacks proceed under the issuer's rules and this Policy's response procedures.

12.4Other jurisdictions.

Mandatory consumer-protection laws of your jurisdiction of residence apply regardless of the Terms of Service's general choice-of-law provisions. This Policy is intended to be consistent with, and does not displace, those mandatory protections.

13Relationship to Dispute Resolution Policy

Refund requests that are uncontested flow through the process described at § 5 without invoking the Dispute Resolution Policy. Contested refund requests are treated as refund disputes under the Dispute Resolution Policy and follow its five-stage ladder. The refund request is the initiating event for Stage 1.

Chargebacks are a separate process operated by the card network; they are not part of the Dispute Resolution Policy. However, the underlying factual dispute that gave rise to the chargeback typically is, and Atlas will continue to process the Dispute Resolution track concurrently with the chargeback response.

14Changes

We may update this Policy to reflect changes in card-network rules, applicable law, or our internal processes. Substantive changes are notified as set out in the Terms of Service. Non-substantive changes may be made without notice.

15Contact

For refund questions, email refunds@atlas.co. For chargeback inquiries, contact your card issuer first, then email refunds@atlas.co so we can participate in the process. Policy questions: legal@atlas.co.

• Related policies
See also.
Process
Dispute Resolution Policy.

The five-stage process that applies to contested refund requests.

Read →
Payment terms
Escrow Terms.

Escrow holding and release mechanics referenced throughout this Policy.

Read →
Governing document
Terms of Service.

Section 7 sets out Fee structure; Section 18 governs indemnification for chargeback-related costs.

Read →

Feb 14, 2026
