v1.7
Oct 22, 2025
Added regional addenda for UK CRA 2015 and California. Clarified Platform-Fee proportional refund formula.
v1.0 – v1.6
Q2–Q3 2025
Archived. Email legal@atlas.co for past versions.

Questions about refunds?

Email refunds@atlas.co. If a chargeback has already been filed, contact your card issuer in parallel so we can respond within the network deadline.

refunds@atlas.co
/ / DMCA & Copyright
// Compliance & governance · copyright notice procedure v1.5

DMCA & Copyright Policy.

EffectiveFebruary 01, 2026
Last updatedFebruary 01, 2026
Versionv1.5
Applies toRights holders & Users
Read time~12 min
Status• Active
• Contents
• The short version

Plain-English summary.

If your copyrighted work appears on Atlas without your permission, send our Designated Agent a notice with the specific elements listed at § 4. We'll take the material down promptly and tell the User who posted it.

If you're the User whose material was taken down and you believe the notice was wrong, you can file a counter-notice under § 6. If the rights holder doesn't sue within 10–14 business days, we restore the material.

Both notices carry penalty-of-perjury attestations. Filing a bad-faith notice exposes you to liability under 17 U.S.C. § 512(f).

This summary is for convenience only. The full policy below is the legally binding version. Where the summary and the full text disagree, the full text controls.

01Introduction & Scope

This DMCA & Copyright Policy (the "Policy") describes how Atlas Talent, Inc. ("Atlas") responds to notices of alleged copyright infringement on the Platform, and how Users whose material has been removed may submit counter-notices. The Policy implements Atlas's obligations and preserves Atlas's safe-harbor protection under the Digital Millennium Copyright Act, 17 U.S.C. § 512 ("DMCA").

This Policy applies to User-generated content on the Platform — profile materials, deliverables exchanged under Contracts, messages, and similar Content. It does not apply to Atlas's own content (site design, documentation, marketing materials), which is protected by copyright in Atlas's name.

02Our Safe Harbor Position

Atlas operates as a "service provider" within the meaning of 17 U.S.C. § 512(k)(1) and acts as an "interactive computer service" under 47 U.S.C. § 230. Content posted by Users on the Platform is User content, not Atlas content.

Atlas's policy is to comply with the DMCA's notice-and-takedown framework and to terminate, in appropriate circumstances, the accounts of Users who are repeat copyright infringers, consistent with 17 U.S.C. § 512(i). Nothing in this Policy constitutes a waiver by Atlas of any position under § 512 or § 230, nor an admission that Atlas has editorial responsibility for User content beyond what is imposed by law.

03Designated Agent

Atlas's DMCA Designated Agent, registered with the U.S. Copyright Office pursuant to 17 U.S.C. § 512(c)(2) and the Office's online directory:

Atlas Talent, Inc.
Attn: DMCA Designated Agent
Elena Kapoor, General Counsel
2261 Market Street #4218
San Francisco, CA 94114
United States

Email: dmca@atlas.co
Phone: +1 (415) 555-0312

U.S. Copyright Office Registration: DMCA-2025-091744
Directory: dmca.copyright.gov

Only notices sent to the Designated Agent at the contact points above are properly served under the DMCA. Notices sent to other Atlas addresses or email accounts may not receive timely attention.

04Notice of Alleged Infringement

A valid DMCA takedown notice must be in writing, addressed to the Designated Agent, and contain the six elements set out in 17 U.S.C. § 512(c)(3)(A). The template below indicates the required content:

• DMCA takedown notice · required elements 17 U.S.C. § 512(c)(3)(A)
01
Signature of rights holder or agent.Required
Physical or electronic signature of a person authorized to act on behalf of the owner of an exclusive right that is allegedly infringed.
A typed name at the end of the notice is sufficient for an electronic signature. Agents should attach evidence of their authority.
02
Identification of the copyrighted work.Required
Describe the copyrighted work claimed to have been infringed. If multiple works are at one site, a representative list is acceptable.
Example: "My article titled Ten Patterns for React State Management published on example.com on March 14, 2025, URL: example.com/articles/react-state".
03
Identification of the infringing material.Required
Identify the material that is claimed to be infringing and information reasonably sufficient to permit Atlas to locate it. URL of the Atlas page is strongly preferred.
Example: "atlas.co/profile/123456 — the portfolio item titled 'React Patterns' is a verbatim copy of my article identified in item 02."
04
Your contact information.Required
Address, telephone number, and email address so Atlas can contact you. The notice-sender must be contactable.
05
Good-faith statement.Required
A statement that you have a good-faith belief that use of the material in the manner complained of is not authorized by the copyright owner, its agent, or the law.
Suggested language: "I have a good-faith belief that the use of the material described above is not authorized by me, my agents, or the law."
06
Accuracy statement (under penalty of perjury).Required
A statement, under penalty of perjury, that the information in the notice is accurate and that you are authorized to act on behalf of the owner of the exclusive right that is allegedly infringed.
Suggested language: "I swear, under penalty of perjury, that the information in this notice is accurate and that I am the copyright owner, or authorized to act on behalf of the owner, of an exclusive right that is allegedly infringed."

A notice that omits any of these elements is not a valid § 512(c)(3) notice. Atlas may decline to act on a deficient notice and may request that the sender resubmit with the missing information. We commonly receive notices that, for example, do not include the accuracy statement under penalty of perjury; these are returned for correction rather than acted upon.

05When We Receive a Notice

When Atlas's Designated Agent receives a valid DMCA notice, we:

  1. Expeditiously remove or disable access to the identified material, consistent with 17 U.S.C. § 512(c)(1)(C). "Expeditiously" is not defined in the statute; our practical benchmark is within 48 business hours of a valid notice, and sooner for clear cases.
  2. Forward the notice (redacted of sensitive contact information) to the User whose material is affected, at the email address on file, along with a copy of this Policy.
  3. Make a record in our DMCA log, including the notice, our action, the affected User's account identifier, and the timestamp.
  4. Notify the User of their right to file a counter-notice under § 6 below.

06Counter-Notice

If material of yours has been removed or disabled in response to a DMCA notice, and you believe the removal was the result of mistake or misidentification, you may submit a counter-notice. A valid counter-notice must contain the four elements set out in 17 U.S.C. § 512(g)(3):

• DMCA counter-notice · required elements 17 U.S.C. § 512(g)(3)
01
Your signature.Required
Your physical or electronic signature.
02
Identification of the removed material.Required
Identify the material removed or to which access was disabled, and the location at which the material appeared before removal.
03
Penalty-of-perjury statement.Required
A statement under penalty of perjury that you have a good-faith belief the material was removed as a result of mistake or misidentification of the material to be removed or disabled.
Suggested language: "I swear, under penalty of perjury, that I have a good-faith belief the material described above was removed or disabled as a result of mistake or misidentification of the material to be removed or disabled."
04
Your name, address, phone, and consent to jurisdiction.Required
Your name, address, telephone number; and a statement that you consent to the jurisdiction of the Federal District Court for the judicial district in which your address is located (or, if your address is outside the United States, the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of California), and that you will accept service of process from the person who filed the DMCA notice, or their agent.

07When We Receive a Counter-Notice

When Atlas's Designated Agent receives a valid counter-notice, we:

  1. Promptly provide a copy to the person who filed the original DMCA notice and inform them that Atlas will replace the removed material or cease disabling access not less than 10 nor more than 14 business days after receipt of the counter-notice, unless the original claimant notifies us that they have filed an action seeking a court order to restrain the User from further engaging in infringing activity;
  2. Restore the material if the original claimant does not file such an action within the 10–14 business day window;
  3. Keep the material disabled if the original claimant notifies us within the window that a court action has been filed, pending the outcome of the court proceeding.

The 10–14 business day window is calculated under 17 U.S.C. § 512(g)(2)(C) and is outside Atlas's discretion to shorten or extend.

08Bad-Faith Notices

⚠ Bad-faith notices · 17 U.S.C. § 512(f)
Misrepresentations are actionable.

Under 17 U.S.C. § 512(f), any person who knowingly materially misrepresents that material is infringing (in a notice) or that material was removed by mistake (in a counter-notice) is liable for damages, including costs and attorneys' fees, incurred by the alleged infringer, the copyright owner, or Atlas as a result of the misrepresentation.

We do not serve as judge or jury on competing claims of authorship or ownership. But when a party concedes facts in correspondence with Atlas that contradict their sworn statement, we may preserve that correspondence and make it available to affected parties and in response to lawful legal process.

09Repeat Infringers

Atlas terminates, in appropriate circumstances, the accounts of Users whom Atlas determines to be repeat copyright infringers, consistent with 17 U.S.C. § 512(i)(1)(A). A "repeat infringer" for these purposes is generally a User against whom Atlas has received valid DMCA notices on three or more separate occasions where no successful counter-notice was filed, subject to Atlas's reasonable judgment on the specific facts.

Appropriate circumstances include the nature of the infringement (willful copying vs. accidental reproduction), the User's response to the notices, and whether the conduct has continued after warning. Atlas does not adopt a strict numerical threshold; the § 512(i) standard is "reasonably implemented," not "mechanically applied."

10International & Non-US Rights Holders

Atlas is a US company. The DMCA notice-and-takedown framework is the primary mechanism available to rights holders, including non-US rights holders whose works are protected under the Berne Convention and related treaties. We accept valid DMCA notices from rights holders in any jurisdiction.

Rights holders in the European Union may also rely on the parallel framework under the EU Copyright Directive (Directive 2019/790), Article 17, and the Digital Services Act (Regulation (EU) 2022/2065), Articles 14–16. For EU rights holders, a notice complying with DSA Article 16 — which requires similar but not identical elements to a DMCA notice — is also accepted. A User who is affected by a removal made under the DSA has access to the DSA's internal-complaint mechanism under Article 20, the out-of-court settlement mechanism under Article 21, and (where applicable) judicial redress.

Rights holders in the United Kingdom may also rely on the notice procedure under the Copyright, Designs and Patents Act 1988, as amended, and the Electronic Commerce (EC Directive) Regulations 2002. We accept valid UK notices.

For notices made under non-US frameworks, use the same Designated Agent contact as for DMCA notices; indicate in the subject line which framework applies (for example, "DSA Article 16 notice").

11Trademark Complaints

Trademark complaints follow a similar but distinct procedure. The DMCA does not apply to trademark infringement; instead, we rely on the Lanham Act and corresponding state and international laws. To submit a trademark complaint:

  1. Email trademarks@atlas.co with the subject line "Trademark complaint";
  2. Identify your trademark (registered or common-law), providing registration numbers where available;
  3. Identify the material on Atlas that you believe infringes;
  4. Explain the basis for the claim — including likelihood of confusion analysis, where relevant; and
  5. Include your contact information and a good-faith statement.

We evaluate trademark complaints individually. Not every complaint results in removal; trademark law supports fair use, nominative use, and legitimate criticism.

12Other Intellectual Property Claims

Claims of patent infringement, trade-secret misappropriation, right-of-publicity violations, and moral rights may be raised with legal@atlas.co. These claims do not fit the § 512 or DSA frameworks and are handled case-by-case under the Terms of Service and applicable law.

13Changes to This Policy

We may update this Policy to reflect changes in applicable law (including amendments to § 512, the DSA, or other frameworks) or in Atlas's processes. Substantive changes are notified as set out in the Terms of Service. The current version is identified at the top of this page; past versions are archived indefinitely.

14Contact

DMCA notices and counter-notices to the Designated Agent at § 3. Trademark complaints to trademarks@atlas.co. Other IP inquiries to legal@atlas.co.

• Related policies
See also.
Conduct policy
Acceptable Use Policy.

Section 7 addresses IP generally and incorporates this DMCA procedure by reference.

Read →
Governing document
Terms of Service.

Sections 9 and 10 govern IP ownership in Platform transactions and content licenses to Atlas.

Read →
Safe harbor
Vulnerability Disclosure.

Good-faith reverse-engineering of security features is protected under the VDP, notwithstanding any DMCA § 1201 claim.

Read →
v1.5 · current
Feb 01, 2026
Substantive. Added DSA Article 16 / EU Copyright Directive Article 17 international framework (§ 10). Refined repeat-infringer language per Cox Communications line of cases. Updated Designated Agent registration number.
v1.4
Sep 18, 2025
Restructured notice/counter-notice templates to match § 512(c)(3) and § 512(g)(3) elements element-by-element.
v1.0 – v1.3
Q2 2025
Archived. Email legal@atlas.co for past versions.

Questions about this policy?

DMCA notices to dmca@atlas.co (or the postal address at § 3). Trademark complaints to trademarks@atlas.co. Other IP or policy questions to legal@atlas.co.

dmca@atlas.co
/ / Accessibility
// Compliance & governance · accessibility commitment v1.2

Accessibility Statement.

EffectiveJanuary 22, 2026
Last updatedJanuary 22, 2026
Versionv1.2
Applies toAll Users
Read time~9 min
Status• Active
• Contents
• The short version

Plain-English summary.

We aim for WCAG 2.2 Level AA conformance across the Atlas platform. As of this Statement's effective date, the platform substantially conforms with WCAG 2.2 AA, with known gaps documented at § 4 and on our remediation roadmap at § 11.

If you encounter an accessibility barrier, or need Atlas in a format we don't currently offer, email accessibility@atlas.co. We respond within two business days and provide an accommodation within fourteen business days absent unusual circumstances.

This summary is for convenience only. The full policy below is the legally binding version. Where the summary and the full text disagree, the full text controls.

01Our Commitment

Atlas is committed to making the Platform usable by the widest possible audience, regardless of ability, sensory capacity, or assistive-technology configuration. Accessibility is not only a legal obligation in most jurisdictions where we operate; it is the right way to run a marketplace whose premise is that talent is distributed everywhere.

This Statement describes the accessibility standards we aim for, the current state of the platform against those standards, the known gaps and our plan to close them, and the procedures by which anyone encountering a barrier can obtain prompt accommodation. We publish this Statement because accuracy beats optimism — a candid statement is more useful and more defensible than a blanket claim of "fully accessible."

02Standards We Follow

We align our work to the following frameworks in order of primacy:

  • Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) 2.2, Level AA — published by the W3C Web Accessibility Initiative in October 2023. This is our primary conformance target.
  • Section 508 of the Rehabilitation Act, § 1194.22 — the federal procurement standard, which incorporates WCAG 2.0 AA by reference. Relevant where Clients are federal agencies or their contractors.
  • EN 301 549 v3.2.1 — the European accessibility standard for ICT, including the European Accessibility Act (Directive (EU) 2019/882), which became enforceable for our category of service on 28 June 2025.
  • ADA Title III — the Americans with Disabilities Act, as applied to "places of public accommodation" and, increasingly, to commercial websites. Informed by the DOJ's April 2024 rule on Title II public-entity web accessibility, which many Title III courts now reference.
  • UK Equality Act 2010 and the UK Public Sector Bodies Accessibility Regulations (for UK public-sector Clients).

03Conformance Status

The table below summarizes our current conformance with WCAG 2.2 Level AA, principle by principle. Met means we conform across the Platform with no known deviations. Partial means we conform in most but not all contexts. Planned means we have a documented plan but are not yet conforming.

1.1
Text alternatives. All non-text content has a text alternative.
Met
1.2
Time-based media. Captions and audio descriptions for video content.
Partial
1.3
Adaptable. Content structure is semantically marked up and programmatically determinable.
Met
1.4
Distinguishable. Color contrast, text resize, reflow, focus appearance. Minor issue in our profile-preview overlay noted at § 4.
Partial
2.1
Keyboard accessible. All functionality is operable through a keyboard alone.
Met
2.2
Enough time. Users can extend or disable time limits on timed interactions.
Met
2.3
Seizures & physical reactions. No content flashes more than 3 times per second.
Met
2.4
Navigable. Users can navigate, locate content, and determine where they are.
Met
2.5
Input modalities. Support for various input methods (touch, stylus, voice, keyboard, switch).
Met
3.1
Readable. Text is readable and understandable. Language is programmatically declared.
Met
3.2
Predictable. Consistent navigation, consistent identification, no unexpected context changes.
Met
3.3
Input assistance. Error identification, labels and instructions, error suggestions.
Partial
4.1
Compatible. Parsing and name/role/value for assistive technology.
Met

04Known Limitations

The following are known accessibility limitations we have identified and are actively working to resolve. We publish them here rather than wait for complaints, because disclosure helps affected users plan and proves our iterative approach to conformance.

  • Assessment interview interface (SC 1.2.4, 1.2.5) — the AI-conducted video interview does not currently provide real-time captions or audio descriptions. Candidates who require captions should request a recruiter-led interview via accessibility@atlas.co; we provide one within 5 business days at no cost.
  • Profile-preview overlay contrast (SC 1.4.3) — text over the hover preview on Candidate cards occasionally falls below the 4.5:1 ratio for certain photo backgrounds. Remediation expected Q2 2026.
  • Error message specificity (SC 3.3.3) — in our payment forms, some validation errors identify the field but do not suggest the specific correction. Remediation in progress; expected Q2 2026.
  • PDF exports — Contract PDFs generated by the Platform are tagged but not fully WCAG 2.2 AA conforming. Alternative HTML versions are available for every Contract on request.
  • Third-party widgets — the Stripe payment iframe and Persona identity-verification flow follow their own accessibility standards. See § 9.

05Assistive Technology Compatibility

We test the Platform with the following assistive technology combinations. Full support means the Platform is operable, perceivable, understandable, and robust on that combination.

  • JAWS 2024+ with Chrome, Firefox, and Edge on Windows — full support
  • NVDA 2024+ with Chrome and Firefox on Windows — full support
  • VoiceOver on macOS 14+ with Safari — full support
  • VoiceOver on iOS 17+ with Safari — full support
  • TalkBack on Android 13+ with Chrome — full support
  • Dragon NaturallySpeaking 16+ — core workflows supported; dictation into time-tracker logging fields is under remediation
  • Switch Control on iOS and macOS — full support
  • ZoomText on Windows — full support up to 400% magnification

If you use a combination not listed here, or encounter an issue on a listed combination, please let us know — we are actively expanding our test matrix.

06Accommodations

If you require an accommodation to use the Platform — whether or not the accommodation is captured by WCAG — email accessibility@atlas.co. Examples of accommodations we routinely provide:

  • Alternative assessment formats (for example, a recruiter-led interview in place of the AI interview, or a text-based assessment in place of a video assessment);
  • Extended time on timed assessments;
  • Alternative methods for identity verification that do not require live video;
  • Receipt of Platform documents (Contracts, invoices) in accessible formats (HTML, tagged PDF, large print, Braille-ready XML);
  • Alternative communication channels for Client–Candidate messaging (email, text-relay services);
  • Navigation assistance by an Atlas support person for a specific workflow.

Accommodation requests are evaluated on an individual-and-interactive basis, consistent with ADA Title III's interactive-process requirement. We respond to accommodation requests within two business days and implement within fourteen business days absent unusual circumstances.

07Alternative Access Channels

Where a specific interface on the Platform is not usable with your assistive technology, we offer alternatives:

  • Phone-assisted workflows for Candidate verification and account setup, by appointment: +1 (833) 555-ATLS;
  • Email-based Contract management for Clients who cannot use the web dashboard; an Atlas support person manages the dashboard on your behalf with your written authorization;
  • In-person or video support sessions with a dedicated accessibility specialist, scheduled through accessibility@atlas.co.

Use of alternative channels does not change the Fee structure and does not disadvantage your use of the Platform in any way.

08Feedback

We want to hear when the Platform works for you and when it doesn't. Feedback routes:

  • Specific barrier report. Email accessibility@atlas.co with a description of the barrier, the URL where it occurs, your assistive-technology setup, and the goal you were trying to accomplish. Two-business-day acknowledgment.
  • General feedback or suggestions. Same inbox; please indicate in the subject whether the message is a barrier report or general feedback.
  • Urgent accommodations in an active engagement. If a barrier is affecting your ability to perform work already under a Contract, include "URGENT" in the subject line for 24-hour acknowledgment.

09Third-Party Content

Portions of the Platform include content or functionality provided by third parties, including payment processors (Stripe), identity-verification providers (Persona, Onfido), video infrastructure (LiveKit), and analytics (PostHog). We evaluate the accessibility of each third-party provider as part of our procurement due diligence and require written accessibility commitments as part of our contracts. We cannot guarantee third-party conformance and treat gaps in third-party accessibility as accommodation matters rather than conformance matters, providing alternatives where a third-party widget creates a barrier.

10Regional Frameworks

Beyond the standards at § 2, the following jurisdiction-specific notices apply:

  • EU Users: this Statement also serves as the declaration required under the European Accessibility Act (Directive (EU) 2019/882) and the implementing national legislation. A machine-readable accessibility statement is published at /.well-known/accessibility-statement.
  • UK Users: this Statement is intended to satisfy the reasonable-adjustments duty under the Equality Act 2010 for Platform use.
  • California residents with disabilities may raise issues under the Unruh Civil Rights Act (Cal. Civ. Code § 51) and the ADA.
  • Canadian Users: we monitor developments under the Accessible Canada Act (federally regulated private sector) and provincial frameworks including Ontario's AODA.

11Our Roadmap

We maintain a rolling accessibility remediation plan. The current priorities are:

  • Q2 2026: close the SC 1.4.3 contrast issue on profile-preview overlay; close the SC 3.3.3 specificity issue on payment-form error messages.
  • Q3 2026: real-time captions (SC 1.2.4) on AI-conducted interviews through integration with our speech-transcription subprocessor.
  • Q4 2026: full WCAG 2.2 AA PDF conformance for auto-generated Contracts.
  • Q1 2027: independent third-party accessibility audit and VPAT publication.

The roadmap may change based on technical feasibility, user feedback priorities, and legal developments. Changes are reflected here and called out in the version history.

12Contact

Accessibility inbox: accessibility@atlas.co. For legal or policy questions about this Statement, email legal@atlas.co. For the formal representative designated under the European Accessibility Act:

EAA Representative:
Atlas Talent EU B.V.
Herengracht 123, 1015 BG Amsterdam, Netherlands
accessibility-eu@atlas.co

• Related policies

Encounter a barrier?

accessibility@atlas.co
/ / Do Not Sell or Share
// Privacy & data · statutory opt-out v1.4

Do Not Sell or Share My
Personal Information.

EffectiveMarch 12, 2026
Last updatedMarch 12, 2026
Versionv1.4
Applies toUS state-law residents
Read time~7 min
Status• Active
• Contents
• The short version

Plain-English summary.

This page is published because California Civil Code § 1798.135 requires it. The short answer is straightforward: we do not sell your personal information, and we do not "share" it for cross-context behavioral advertising.

You can still submit a formal opt-out for the record, and we honor Global Privacy Control (GPC) signals automatically. California residents also have the Right to Limit Use of Sensitive Personal Information; instructions below.

This page is the statutory opt-out. The full Privacy Policy is the legally binding Policy. Where this page and the Privacy Policy disagree, the Privacy Policy controls.

01Our Position

• Our position · for the record
We do not sell your
personal information.

We do not sell personal information for monetary or other valuable consideration. We do not "share" personal information for cross-context behavioral advertising. We have not done so in the preceding twelve (12) months, and we have no plans to do so. This is a firm, written representation, not a marketing claim.

This page is published because California Civil Code § 1798.135 requires businesses that sell or share personal information to publish a "Do Not Sell or Share My Personal Information" link. Our circumstances do not trigger that requirement, but we publish this page anyway because:

  • It provides a clear, accessible statement of our position for California residents and other state-law residents who expect to see it;
  • It gives us a place to record our commitment in a way a future Atlas could not quietly walk back;
  • It provides the mechanics for submitting a formal opt-out request for anyone who wants one on record with us.

02What "Sale" and "Sharing" Mean Under These Laws

Under the California Consumer Privacy Act as amended by the California Privacy Rights Act ("CCPA/CPRA"):

  • "Sale" (Cal. Civ. Code § 1798.140(ad)) means selling, renting, releasing, disclosing, disseminating, making available, transferring, or otherwise communicating orally, in writing, or by electronic or other means, a consumer's personal information by the business to a third party for monetary or other valuable consideration.
  • "Sharing" (Cal. Civ. Code § 1798.140(ah)) means making personal information available to a third party for cross-context behavioral advertising, whether or not for monetary or other valuable consideration.

Service-provider relationships, where a third party processes personal information on Atlas's behalf under a written data-processing agreement that restricts the third party's use, are not "sale" or "sharing" under these definitions. Our subprocessors — listed at the Subprocessors List — operate under such agreements.

03Your Right to Opt Out

California Civil Code § 1798.120 gives California consumers the right to direct a business not to sell or share their personal information. Analogous rights exist in the state-law frameworks listed at § 10.

Because we do not sell or share, there is nothing operationally to stop. You may still submit a formal opt-out request, which we will acknowledge and file with a confirmation for your records. This provides you with documentary evidence of our no-sale/no-share position, enforceable as a written representation.

04Sensitive Personal Information

California Civil Code § 1798.121 gives consumers the Right to Limit Use and Disclosure of Sensitive Personal Information ("SPI"). SPI includes categories such as government identifiers, account credentials, precise geolocation, racial or ethnic origin, health information, sexual orientation, and — relevant to Atlas — biometric information processed for the purpose of uniquely identifying a consumer.

Atlas uses SPI only for the purposes set out in the Privacy Policy and the Biometric Consent Policy, all of which qualify for the § 7027(m) service-performance exception. Consequently, the Right to Limit does not require Atlas to change any practice.

That said, you may submit a Right-to-Limit request for the record. For biometric SPI specifically, the Biometric Consent Policy provides a withdrawal mechanism at its § 10 that actually removes biometric templates from our systems.

05Global Privacy Control (GPC)

We respect the Global Privacy Control signal as specified at globalprivacycontrol.org. When we detect a valid GPC signal from your browser or device, we treat it as a legally effective opt-out of sale and sharing — even though we do not sell or share — and we record the signal for compliance audit purposes.

A GPC signal is applied per device. To ensure the signal reaches us from every browser you use, set GPC in each browser. See our Cookie Policy § 8 for more on how we handle browser-level signals.

06How to Submit a Request

You have three ways to submit a Do Not Sell or Share request:

  1. GPC signal — enable Global Privacy Control in your browser. We receive the signal automatically; no form submission needed. This is the fastest method.
  2. Email — send a message to privacy@atlas.co with the subject line "Do Not Sell or Share opt-out". Include your Atlas account email and your state of residence so we can route the request correctly.
  3. Web form — from your account settings, navigate to Privacy → State Rights → Do Not Sell or Share. Submit the pre-filled form.

For a Right-to-Limit-SPI request (California only), use the same channels with the subject line "Limit SPI". For other state-law rights, see § 10.

07Authorized Agents

You may designate an authorized agent to submit a request on your behalf. An authorized agent must provide:

  1. Written authorization from you, signed and dated, specifying the agent's identity and the rights the agent is authorized to exercise;
  2. The agent's contact information, including email and mailing address;
  3. For California CCPA/CPRA purposes, evidence that the agent is a legal entity authorized to conduct business in California (where applicable), consistent with 11 Cal. Code Regs. § 7063.

Atlas may contact you directly to verify the authorization before acting on the agent's request. If we cannot verify the agent within fifteen (15) business days, we will notify both you and the agent that the request cannot be processed.

08Verification & Timing

We verify the identity of the requestor before processing any opt-out request. Verification typically uses at least two of: (a) confirmation via the email address on file; (b) a recent account action (log-in, payment, message) performed before the request; (c) a response to a security question we have on file. For requests from non-account holders, we accept alternative verification consistent with 11 Cal. Code Regs. § 7060.

We respond to Do Not Sell or Share requests within the statutory windows:

  • Acknowledgement: within 10 business days of receipt;
  • Substantive response: within 45 calendar days, with one 45-day extension available in complex cases (notified in the acknowledgement).

09Non-Discrimination

We will not discriminate against you for exercising any right under this page, consistent with Cal. Civ. Code § 1798.125. We do not charge different prices or provide different service levels based on whether you have submitted an opt-out request. This is not a hypothetical commitment: because we do not sell or share to begin with, there is no operational action to take — and therefore no operational disincentive we might impose on you.

10Multi-State Applicability

Analogous opt-out rights exist in the following U.S. state privacy laws. Atlas extends the same treatment to residents of each state: we do not sell or share personal information, and we honor GPC signals and formal opt-out requests. Specific state rights beyond the opt-out are handled through the Privacy Policy § 15.

• California
CCPA / CPRA.
Cal. Civ. Code §§ 1798.100–199.100

Original framework; GPC-binding. Private right of action for data breaches only (§ 1798.150).

• Colorado
Colorado Privacy Act.
C.R.S. 6-1-1301 et seq.

Opt-out of sale, targeted advertising, and profiling. GPC-binding (universal opt-out mechanism).

• Connecticut
CTDPA.
Conn. Gen. Stat. § 42-515 et seq.

Analogous opt-out framework. Enforced by CT AG. GPC-binding from January 2025.

• Virginia
VCDPA.
Va. Code § 59.1-575 et seq.

First-in-effect non-California state law (2023). Opt-out of sale, targeted ads, profiling.

• Texas
TDPSA.
Tex. Bus. & Com. Code ch. 541

Broad applicability; effective July 2024. AG enforcement; no private right of action.

• Utah
UCPA.
Utah Code § 13-61-101 et seq.

Narrower than most; opt-out of sale and targeted ads. No profiling opt-out.

• Oregon
OCPA.
ORS 646A.570 et seq.

Effective July 2024. Opt-out of sale, targeted ads, profiling. GPC-binding.

• Montana
MCDPA.
Mont. Code § 30-14-2801 et seq.

Effective October 2024. Analogous opt-out. AG enforcement.

• Delaware
DPDPA.
6 Del. C. § 12D

Effective January 2025. Among the broadest in scope; lower thresholds.

• New Hampshire
NHDPA.
N.H. Rev. Stat. ch. 507-H

Effective January 2025. Opt-out of sale, targeted ads, profiling.

• New Jersey
NJDPA.
N.J.S.A. 56:8-166.4 et seq.

Effective January 2025. Includes "financial info" as sensitive; GPC-binding.

• Other states
Indiana, Iowa, Kentucky, Maryland, Minnesota, Rhode Island, Tennessee.
Effective 2025–2026

Each has analogous opt-out frameworks. Residents of these states may submit opt-out requests through the channels at § 6.

11Changes to This Page

We may update this page to reflect new state privacy frameworks or changes in enforcement guidance. Any material change — in particular, any change to our no-sale/no-share position — would be treated as a substantive change under the Privacy Policy and would require 30 days' advance notice to affected Users. The current version is identified at the top of this page.

12Contact

Do Not Sell or Share opt-out requests: privacy@atlas.co. Data-protection inquiries generally: same inbox. Attention: Data Protection Officer.

• Related policies
See also.
Privacy
Privacy Policy.

The canonical privacy document. Section 15 contains the regional addenda that this page supplements.

Read →
Tracking
Cookie Policy.

How we treat GPC signals at the browser level, and the category-level preferences center.

Read →
Biometric
Biometric Consent.

For biometric SPI, use the withdrawal mechanism at Section 10 of that Policy for actual deletion.

Read →

Submit a request?

The fastest way is to enable Global Privacy Control in your browser. For a formal record, email privacy@atlas.co with the subject "Do Not Sell or Share opt-out." Standard 45-day response window.
